guides.title: Best GPS Dog Tracker UK 2026
guides.summary: Lost dogs are not a small problem. We looked at four trackers — live GPS, subscription costs, collar weight, and battery life — to find the one most UK dog owners will actually keep using.
guides.heroImageAlt: Boxer dog running freely through a forest path

faq.q: Do GPS trackers for dogs work without a subscription?
faq.a: Most live-GPS trackers require a monthly subscription for cellular data. Bluetooth-only tags like Apple AirTag have no subscription but only show last-known location, not real-time movement — they rely on someone nearby with an iPhone. For a dog that can run far, a live tracker with a subscription is the safer choice.

faq.q: How accurate are dog GPS trackers in the UK?
faq.a: Most use a combination of GPS, Wi-Fi, and cellular triangulation. In open areas accuracy is typically 2–9 metres. In dense city streets or thick woodland, expect 10–30 metres. Tractive, which has the largest UK user base, updates location every 2–3 seconds in Live Tracking mode.

faq.q: What is a safe GPS tracker weight for small dogs?
faq.a: The general guideline is that a collar attachment should be no more than 5% of the dog's body weight. The Tractive GPS DOG 6 weighs 35g, which is fine for most dogs over 4kg. For toy breeds under 4kg, look for trackers under 15g — options are limited but improving.

body.text: A dog that slips a lead or finds a gap in the fence can be a mile away in under ten minutes. GPS trackers do not prevent that — but they shrink the search from hours to minutes. The question is which one is worth the subscription.
What we looked at

We evaluated four options against the same criteria: how quickly the location updates, how long the battery lasts on a real walk, whether the app is usable in a panic, and what the subscription actually costs over a year. We also looked at whether the device is too heavy or bulky for smaller breeds.
Our top pick: Tractive GPS DOG 6

The Tractive GPS DOG 6 is our pick for most UK dog owners. It updates location every 2–3 seconds in Live Tracking mode, works across the whole of the UK and most of Europe on a single subscription, and the app is genuinely easy to use when you're stressed. At 35g it fits anything labrador-sized and up without issue. The subscription costs around £4–5 per month. That is money you spend once and forget about; the moment it pays for itself is the first time you actually need it.
What about Apple AirTag?

The AirTag collar holder is popular because AirTag itself costs very little and there is no subscription. The catch: AirTag is Bluetooth, not cellular GPS. It relies on other iPhone users walking near your dog to update the location. In a busy city park that can work. On a quiet country road it can show the same location for hours. We recommend it as a backup layer for iPhone owners, not as a primary tracker for dogs that might travel far.
The bottom line

If your dog is an escape risk — reactive, a sight hound, or simply very fast — pay for live GPS. The Tractive subscription runs to about £50–60 a year. Compared to a vet bill, a replacement dog, or 12 hours of searching in the dark, it is not an expensive insurance policy.

guides.title: Cat Water Fountain Buying Guide 2026
guides.summary: Cats evolved to avoid still water — their kidneys pay the price when they do not drink enough. We break down what actually matters when choosing a fountain: material, noise, filter type, and how easy it is to clean.
guides.heroImageAlt: Cat drinking from a flowing pet water fountain

faq.q: Are stainless steel cat fountains better than plastic?
faq.a: For most cats, yes. Plastic fountains can harbour bacteria in micro-scratches over time, and some cats develop feline acne from plastic bowls. Stainless steel is easier to sanitise, does not absorb odours, and lasts longer. The trade-off is cost: a decent stainless fountain costs £40–70 vs £20–35 for a good plastic model.

faq.q: How often should I clean a cat water fountain?
faq.a: The pump and bowl need a full clean at least once a week. Filters need replacing every 2–4 weeks depending on the model and how many cats use it. Cats with kidney disease or cats that shed heavily may need more frequent filter changes. This is the main chore people underestimate when they buy a fountain.

faq.q: Why does my cat ignore the water fountain?
faq.a: A few common reasons: the fountain is too close to the food bowl (cats prefer water sources away from their kill in the wild), the water is not circulating enough to catch the cat's attention, or the pump noise is bothering them. Try moving it to a different room and running it on the higher flow setting for a few days.

body.text: Most vets will tell you that getting a cat to drink more is one of the best preventative measures you can take for kidney health. A water fountain is not a gadget — it is a practical tool. The trick is picking one that you will actually clean weekly.
Plastic vs stainless vs ceramic

Plastic is cheap and widely available. The problem is that plastic scratches, and those scratches harbour bacteria and biofilm that a regular rinse will not remove. If your cat has ever developed chin acne, a plastic bowl or fountain is a likely culprit. Stainless steel is our preferred material: it is dishwasher-safe, non-porous, and does not absorb odours. Ceramic is beautiful and hygienic but fragile and usually expensive. For most owners, stainless is the right call.
Our top pick: Petlibro Stainless Steel Fountain

The Petlibro stainless fountain hits the balance between price, hygiene, and quiet operation better than most. The pump is whisper-quiet (important for nervous cats), the stainless bowl disassembles fully for cleaning, and the filter is easy to source and replace. Thousands of owners report cats that started drinking noticeably more within a week of switching. That is the metric that actually matters.
Budget option: Catit Flower Fountain

The Catit Flower Fountain is the plastic option we would recommend if budget is the constraint. It has a three-flow design that many cats find irresistible, and the raised drinking surface suits cats with flat faces. Clean it properly and replace the filter regularly and it will serve most cats well. Just do not skip the weekly clean.
The one thing most buyers get wrong

People buy a fountain, the cat uses it once or twice, then it gets ignored. Usually this is because the fountain is placed next to the food bowl — cats instinctively prefer water sources away from their food. Move it to another room entirely. Give the cat a week to discover it on its own terms. Most cats come around.

guides.title: Best Pet Camera for a Small Flat 2026
guides.summary: When you have one room and a dog that makes decisions the moment you leave, a camera matters. We looked at no-subscription options, wide-angle lenses, and app reliability — so you can watch without paying forever.
guides.heroImageAlt: Brown dog sitting alone on a leather sofa at home

faq.q: Which pet cameras work without a subscription?
faq.a: Blink Mini and Kasa KC410S both offer free local storage (Blink via USB drive, Kasa via micro-SD card). You get motion alerts and live view without paying monthly. Petcube has a free tier with limited cloud clips. Furbo requires a subscription for most useful features. If you want no ongoing costs, Blink or Kasa are your best options.

faq.q: Do I need a pan-tilt camera for a small flat?
faq.a: Not necessarily. In a studio or one-bedroom flat, a wide-angle fixed camera placed in a corner often covers enough of the room. Pan-tilt becomes more useful in L-shaped rooms or open-plan spaces where a single angle misses important areas. The Kasa KC410S covers most small rooms without needing to move.

faq.q: Can my pet hear me through a pet camera?
faq.a: Most cameras have two-way audio — you can speak through the app and hear the pet. Some dogs find the owner's voice through a speaker reassuring; others find it confusing and bark more. Try it for a few days and see which category your pet falls into before deciding if it helps.

body.text: A flat with limited space needs a camera that covers a lot of ground without becoming a permanent fixture on the coffee table. Most people want to check in, not manage a security system. We focused on cameras that are quick to set up, easy to use daily, and do not require a paid subscription to be useful.
What matters most in a small space

Wide-angle lens coverage (130° or more), reliable motion alerts, clear audio, and a free or cheap cloud plan. Night vision matters if your pet is active after dark. Two-way audio is nice for dogs; most cats ignore it. Treat dispensing (Furbo, Petcube Bites) is genuinely useful for separation anxiety training but adds cost.
Our top pick for a small flat: Kasa Smart Indoor Camera KC410S

The Kasa KC410S is our first recommendation because it covers a full room with its pan-tilt 2K sensor, sets up in under ten minutes, works with Alexa and Google Home, and costs nothing beyond the device price for basic live view and local storage. For renters who do not want to drill anything into walls, it sits on a shelf and covers nearly everything. The app is stable and the motion alerts are reliable without being constant.
Best no-subscription option: Blink Mini

The Blink Mini is small, cheap, and genuinely usable without paying Blink anything monthly if you plug in a USB drive. It does not have pan-tilt, but at under £30 it is a reasonable first camera for someone who wants to test whether a camera helps before committing to something more expensive. The motion zones help cut down on pointless alerts when a curtain moves.
If you want treat dispensing: Furbo 360

The Furbo 360 Dog Camera rotates 360 degrees and fires treats. If you are actively working on separation anxiety and need to be able to intervene and reward calm behaviour remotely, it is worth the premium. Be aware that the subscription is required for most of the smarter features like barking alerts and activity notifications. Budget for both device and subscription when you price it up.
